Client 2: Yeah, and just make sure we’re not missing something obvious. Simple version is fine for me.
Advisor: Perfect. I’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income are you two bringing in per year, all-in?
Client 1: All-in household gross is 230050 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s across salary, business income, and rental income, right?
Client 1: Yes. There’s salary monthly, plus my business income streams and rental income monthly.

Advisor: Okay. On the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses run—just the household burn rate?
Client 2: Ballpark it’s around eight grand a month.
Advisor: Okay—when you say eight, is it closer to 7,900 or closer to 8,500? Just so we’re not drifting.
Client 1: It’s 7900 per month. Thomas is rounding.
Client 2: Yeah, 7900. I round everything, sorry.

Client 2: Yeah, and just make sure we’re not missing something obvious. Simple version is fine for me.

Client 1: Yes. There’s salary monthly, plus my business income streams and rental income monthly.

Client 2: No, we don’t have any. Monthly debt payments total is 0.
Advisor: Great. So no loans outstanding—clean slate there. Let me shift to assets—what you’ve got invested and accessible. Roughly what are your investable assets total, excluding the house?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 800400.
Advisor: Thanks. And we’ll break that down in a second. Before we do—your home is separate from that, correct? You’re not counting the primary residence in the 800400.
Client 1: Correct. The primary residence is separate—value is 806550.

Client 2: I always mix this up—are you asking the state one or the federal one?
Advisor: Federal bracket band—your marginal federal bracket. State we can layer in later.
Client 1: Federal bracket band is 32%.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Let me just jot that down.
Advisor: And since we’re here, I’m going to do a quick sweep so I’m not missing anything from what you already told me: income, assets, debts, and insurance.

Client 2: Yeah, and just make sure we’re not missing something obvious. Simple version is fine for me.

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi Thomas—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. We’re 55, and we want to sanity-check retirement timing and taxes—like, let’s level-set and quantify it.
Client 2: Yeah, and just make sure we’re not missing something obvious. Simple version is fine for me.
Advisor: Perfect. I’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income are you two bringing in per year, all-in?
Client 1: All-in household gross is 230050 a year.

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi Thomas—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. We’re 55, and we want to sanity-check retirement timing and taxes—like, let’s level-set and quantify it.
Client 2: Yeah, and just make sure we’re not missing something obvious. Simple version is fine for me.
Advisor: Perfect. I’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income are you two bringing in per year, all-in?
Client 1: All-in household gross is 230050 a year.

Advisor: Okay, moderate—thank you. Patricia, does that match how you think about it?
Client 1: Yes—moderate. I’m fine taking risk if we’ve got guardrails and we’re not forced to sell at the wrong time.
Advisor: Makes sense. Last admin-ish one: how are you engaging with an advisor—are we talking a registered investment advisor relationship, or something like a brokerage setup?
Client 2: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great—thanks. And just so I’m not missing anything from earlier, you both are employed right now, correct?

Client 1: We’re married.
Advisor: Got it. Next—what state are you filing from most of the time? Just the state abbreviation is fine.
Client 2: TX.
Advisor: Perfect. And risk-wise—when markets swing around, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, or aggressive? I know that’s a little squishy, but your gut reaction helps.
Client 2: I’d say moderate. I don’t love big drops, but we’re not hiding in cash either.

Advisor: Got it. Next—what state are you filing from most of the time? Just the state abbreviation is fine.
Client 2: TX.
Advisor: Perfect. And risk-wise—when markets swing around, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, or aggressive? I know that’s a little squishy, but your gut reaction helps.
Client 2: I’d say moderate. I don’t love big drops, but we’re not hiding in cash either.
Advisor: Okay, moderate—thank you. Patricia, does that match how you think about it?

Client 2: Correct—none right now.
Advisor: Okay. That gives me a clean baseline. Next time we’ll plug in the retirement ages you’re considering and stress-test a “bad year” scenario like you mentioned, Thomas.
Advisor: Alright—before we end today, I want to capture a few household-level items that drive how I set up the plan. Patricia, Thomas—first one: are you two married, or is it a different legal status?
Client 1: We’re married.
Advisor: Got it. Next—what state are you filing from most of the time? Just the state abbreviation is fine.

Client 1: Yes—primary.
Advisor: Got it. And Patricia, what’s your gross annual income number that we should anchor to? I know we talked streams, but I want the top-line figure.
Client 1: My gross annual income is 156200.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. And for your work, I’m going to bucket it into an occupation group—would you describe your role as executive level, like an exec role?
Client 1: Yes, exec.

Advisor: Great. So no loans outstanding—clean slate there. Let me shift to assets—what you’ve got invested and accessible. Roughly what are your investable assets total, excluding the house?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 800400.
Advisor: Thanks. And we’ll break that down in a second. Before we do—your home is separate from that, correct? You’re not counting the primary residence in the 800400.
Client 1: Correct. The primary residence is separate—value is 806550.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. Let’s unpack the investable accounts. Thomas, do you have a taxable investment account—something not in a retirement wrapper?

Advisor: Got it. And Patricia, what’s your gross annual income number that we should anchor to? I know we talked streams, but I want the top-line figure.
Client 1: My gross annual income is 156200.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. And for your work, I’m going to bucket it into an occupation group—would you describe your role as executive level, like an exec role?
Client 1: Yes, exec.
Advisor: Great. Last tiny admin item: in my notes I label you two as Client 1 and Client 2 just so I don’t mix accounts—Patricia, you’re Client 1, correct?

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi Thomas—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. We’re 55, and we want to sanity-check retirement timing and taxes—like, let’s level-set and quantify it.
Client 2: Yeah, and just make sure we’re not missing something obvious. Simple version is fine for me.
Advisor: Perfect. I’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income are you two bringing in per year, all-in?

Advisor: Great. On income sources—just to make sure I’m not missing a stream—Patricia, you mentioned business income and rental income monthly, plus there’s a salary line that’s joint. Does that sound right?
Client 1: Yes. I have business income of 35,300 annually, rental income of 52,300 annually, and another business income of 102,100 annually. And we have joint salary of 40,350 annually.
Advisor: That’s helpful—thank you. Thomas, are you employed as well, or is your income embedded in that joint salary line?
Client 2: I’m employed, but yeah, it’s basically captured in the salary piece the way we track it.
Advisor: Okay. Last category—protection. Any life insurance policies, disability coverage, or long-term care policies we should factor in today?

Client 2: Yes, I’m employed.
Advisor: Got it. And what should we use as your gross annual income—just the top-line number?
Client 2: Ballpark it’s mid-70s, but the exact gross annual income is 73850.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And for planning purposes I also tag an occupation group—what field are you in, Thomas? Like healthcare, tech, education…?
Client 2: Education.

Advisor: Got it. And what should we use as your gross annual income—just the top-line number?
